title: Composition and Props as Component APIs

learningObjectives.text: Apply component composition using a new example and justify the result.
Apply avoiding prop drilling through composition using a new example and justify the result.
Apply designing a clear component api using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns composition and props as component apis from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Component Design and Reusability** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Component composition** → **Avoiding prop drilling through composition** → **Designing a clear component API**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Component composition
sections.body: Composition builds behavior by nesting and combining components instead of relying on inheritance.

sections.title: Avoiding prop drilling through composition
sections.body: Before adding context, consider moving the consumer closer to the owner or passing a configured element through intermediate components.

sections.title: Designing a clear component API
sections.body: A clear component API uses intent-based names, sensible defaults, few contradictory options, and explicit composition points.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Named props and children form a clear component API.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Composition and Props as Component APIs connects Component composition, Avoiding prop drilling through composition, Designing a clear component API.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Component composition” in the context of Composition and Props as Component APIs?

choices.label: A component definition is the function or class. Each rendered position represents an instance with its own state identity.

choices.label: Evaluate alternatives by complexity, clarity, testability, performance, team understanding, and likely change rather than searching for a universally perfect pattern.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Composition and Props as Component APIs in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Component composition deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Composition and Props as Component APIs?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Composition and Props as Component APIs.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Composition and Props as Component APIs.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Composition and Props as Component APIs.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Component composition, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Composition and Props as Component APIs?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.label: Avoid Composition and Props as Component APIs completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.label: Use Composition and Props as Component APIs when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
